perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, CompuCom and Seller have entered into an Asset Purchase Agreement dated as of May 10 , 1999 (the "Asset Purchase Agreement"); WHEREAS, the execution of this Agreement is a condition to CompuCom acquiring, and Seller disposing of, the Purchased Assets (as defined in the Asset Purchase Agreement) in connection with the Asset Purchase Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, CompuCom and ENTEX have entered into an Asset Purchase Agreement dated as of May 10 , 1999 (the "Asset Purchase Agreement"); WHEREAS, the execution of this Agreement is a condition to CompuCom acquiring, and ENTEX disposing of, the Purchased Assets (as defined in the Asset Purchase Agreement) in connection with the Asset Purchase Agreement;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Seller' to 'ENTEX' in the recitals creates an in-text contradiction because 'Seller' is defined as ENTEX Information Services, Inc. throughout the agreement and is used in the operative clauses. This creates confusion on whether obligations apply to ENTEX Information Services, Inc., or any seller.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Seller' to 'ENTEX' in the recitals directly contradicts section 3.1 which refers to obligations of the 'Seller'. Since 'Seller' is now ambiguous, it creates confusion and makes enforcement of section 3.1 unclear. The term 'Seller' in the original text had a clear definition, but the change introduces ambiguity regarding who is bound by the agreement in 3.1.

perturbation.original_text: (3) "Product" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.changed_text: (3) "Goods" means any computer or related hardware and peripherals ------- (including hubs, switches and routers or networking hardware) or software products (including networking software products) which CompuCom has the ability to sell.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Product' to 'Goods' introduces ambiguity. Throughout the agreement, 'Product' is used with specific meaning, especially in Article III regarding the non-compete agreement. By redefining 'Product' as 'Goods' here, it creates confusion as to which term applies where. It also creates contradiction, as product business has it's own definition
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non- ------------------ Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Product' to 'Goods' creates a contradiction. The original text specifically defined 'Product,' which is later used in the non-compete agreement in section 3.1. By changing 'Product' to 'Goods' in the definition, it introduces ambiguity as to whether the non-compete agreement applies to 'Goods' or still refers to the originally defined 'Product'. This ambiguity directly impacts the enforceability of the non-compete clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction according to the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition because the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them. This contradicts the Seller's agreement in section 3.1.

perturbation.original_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, ------------------------------------- system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) "Managed Services" means (a) consulting, ------------------------------------- system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' to 'Managed Services' changes the contractual scope. The agreement uses 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' in specific obligations and permissions, so it becomes uncertain to refer to 'Managed Services' instead, leading to conflicting interpretations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' to 'Managed Services' creates a contradiction. The contradicted text explicitly refers to 'Outsourcing and Professional Services' when outlining obligations for the seller regarding future service customers. By changing the defined term, the agreement no longer clearly defines which services the seller must consider when influencing customers, creating a direct conflict and impacting the enforceability of the obligation, which aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition and the provided explanation.

perturbation.original_text: (6) "Service Accounts" means the customer accounts of Seller listed ---------------- on Exhibits B and C hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: (6) "Client Accounts" means the customer accounts of Seller listed ---------------- on Exhibits B and C hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Service Accounts' to 'Client Accounts' leads to confusion. 'Service Accounts' are specifically referred to in the non-compete agreement (Article III). The change raises the question of whether these are the same accounts, creating conflicts in enforcing the agreement's restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Service Accounts' to 'Client Accounts' creates a contradiction because the non-compete agreement in the 'Contradicted Text' specifically refers to 'Service Accounts.' By changing the definition, it becomes unclear whether the non-compete agreement applies to 'Client Accounts,' thus creating a direct conflict regarding the enforceability of the non-compete clause. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' where the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, -------- but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term -------- "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.changed_text: (7) "IT Solutions" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, -------- but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term -------- "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.explanation: Renaming 'Services' to 'IT Solutions' while keeping the exclusion referring to Services introduce contradictions because now, the term that determines the exclusions refers to services when the term IT solutions refers to solutions, creating confussion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) CompuCom agrees that during the Non- -------------------- Competition Period CompuCom will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit B hereto; --------- (ii) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit C hereto except to the extent provided for in such Exhibit; or (iii) except as contemplated by the Asset Purchase Agreement, solicit, entice or induce any employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller to terminate his or her employment with Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Services' and then excludes certain activities related to CompuCom. The changed text renames 'Services' to 'IT Solutions' but keeps the exclusions referencing 'Services'. The contradicted text then refers to 'Services' regarding non-competition. Since the definition of 'Services' (as it relates to the non-competition clause) is now unclear due to the changed definition focusing on 'IT Solutions' with exclusions still for the original term 'Services', there is now a contradiction. It's unclear if the non-competition agreement applies to 'IT Solutions' or to the activities that are now excluded from 'IT Solutions' but previously were part of 'Services'.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Goods Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Goods for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Goods is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Goods offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Products, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Product' to 'Goods' and keeping one of them the original text introduces ambiguity. The term 'Product' now clashes with 'Goods' making it difficult to ascertain which term accurately reflects the parties' intention, creating room for multiple interpretations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Goods Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Goods for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Goods is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Goods offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Products, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves changing 'Product' to 'Goods' while inconsistently using both terms within the same Article IV. This misaligned terminology directly contradicts the expectation of consistent and clear terminology. The presence of both 'Product' and 'Goods' without clear distinction creates ambiguity, making it difficult to ascertain the parties' intention, which aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'. The contradicted text is Article IV, highlighting the use of both terms which creates an in-text contradiction.
